modules.title: Arrays & Hashing

sections.heading: Two Sum Pattern
sections.content: Given an array find two numbers that add to target. Brute force is O(N squared). Hash map makes it O(N). This is the #1 most asked coding question across all companies globally.
sections.tip: Whenever you see find pair with sum/difference — think Hash Map. O(N) vs O(N²) is the difference between getting hired or rejected.

sections.heading: Frequency Counter Pattern
sections.content: Count occurrences using hash map. Solves anagram checking duplicate finding and majority element problems.
sections.tip: Frequency counter solves: anagrams duplicates majority element unique characters. Build a map count occurrences then query it.

sections.heading: Array Manipulation
sections.content: Common array patterns that appear in every coding test.
sections.tip: Kadane's Algorithm for max subarray is asked in TCS Infosys Wipro. Learn it by heart: current = max(element, current+element), maxSum = max(maxSum, current).

key_points: Hash Map turns O(N²) into O(N)
Frequency counter for counting occurrences
Two pointer for sorted array problems
Kadane's for maximum subarray sum
Always think: can a hash map help?

module_quiz.question: Two Sum optimal approach?
module_quiz.explanation: Store complement in map. One pass O(N) vs O(N²) brute force.

module_quiz.question: Anagram check uses?
module_quiz.explanation: Count character frequencies. Both strings should have same counts.

module_quiz.question: Kadane's Algorithm solves?
module_quiz.explanation: Finds contiguous subarray with largest sum in single pass.

module_quiz.question: Remove duplicates from sorted array?
module_quiz.explanation: Slow pointer tracks unique position fast pointer scans ahead.

module_quiz.question: Hash Map lookup time?
module_quiz.explanation: Hash maps provide constant time average lookup.

modules.title: Two Pointers

sections.heading: Two Pointer Pattern
sections.content: Use two pointers moving toward each other or in same direction. Works on sorted arrays. Eliminates nested loops turning O(N²) into O(N).
sections.tip: Two Pointer works when: array is sorted or you need to find pairs. Sort first then use two pointers from both ends. Three Sum = fix one element then Two Sum on rest.

key_points: Two pointers from both ends for sorted arrays
Eliminates nested loops O(N²) to O(N)
Sort first if array is unsorted
Three Sum = fix one + Two Sum on rest
Palindrome check = two pointers from ends

module_quiz.question: Two pointer works best on?
module_quiz.explanation: Sorted order lets pointers decide which direction to move.

module_quiz.question: Two pointer time complexity?
module_quiz.explanation: Single pass with two pointers = linear time.

module_quiz.question: Three Sum approach?
options: Fix one element + two pointer on rest
module_quiz.explanation: Sort array. Fix i then two pointer on remaining. O(N²) vs O(N³).

module_quiz.question: Palindrome two pointer?
options: Compare from both ends moving inward
module_quiz.explanation: Left and right pointers compare characters moving toward center.

module_quiz.question: Container with most water uses?
module_quiz.explanation: Move the shorter side inward to potentially find larger container.

modules.title: Sliding Window

sections.heading: Sliding Window Pattern
sections.content: Maintain a window of elements that slides through the array. Used for subarray and substring problems. Fixed size window or variable size window.
sections.tip: Sliding window = whenever you see contiguous subarray or substring with some condition. Fixed window: add right remove left. Variable window: expand right shrink left.

key_points: Fixed window: size stays same slides forward
Variable window: expand right shrink left
Used for subarray substring problems
O(N) time instead of O(N²)
Track window state with hash map or variables

module_quiz.question: Sliding window solves?
module_quiz.explanation: Any problem asking about contiguous elements with a condition.

module_quiz.question: Fixed window technique?
options: Add right element remove left element
module_quiz.explanation: Slide by adding new element and removing old. Constant time per step.

module_quiz.question: Variable window shrinks when?
module_quiz.explanation: Expand until condition met then shrink from left to find minimum.

module_quiz.question: Longest substring no repeats uses?
options: Sliding window + hash map for last seen positions
module_quiz.explanation: Window tracks characters. When repeat found move left past previous occurrence.

module_quiz.question: Sliding window time complexity?
module_quiz.explanation: Each element is added and removed at most once. Linear time.

modules.title: Stack & Queue Patterns

sections.heading: Stack Patterns
sections.content: LIFO structure. Used for matching brackets next greater element and monotonic stack problems.
sections.tip: See parentheses/brackets → Stack. See next greater/smaller → Monotonic Stack. These two patterns cover 90% of stack interview questions.

key_points: Stack for matching brackets
Monotonic stack for next greater/smaller
Min Stack uses auxiliary stack
Stack converts recursion to iteration
LIFO last in first out

module_quiz.question: Valid parentheses uses?
options: Stack — push open pop for close
module_quiz.explanation: Push opening brackets. Pop and match when closing bracket found.

module_quiz.question: Monotonic stack solves?
options: Next greater or next smaller element
module_quiz.explanation: Maintains stack in increasing or decreasing order to find next greater/smaller.

module_quiz.question: Min Stack getMin is?
module_quiz.explanation: Second stack tracks minimum at each level. Pop both together.

module_quiz.question: Stack is?
module_quiz.explanation: Last element pushed is first one popped.

module_quiz.question: Stack used for?
module_quiz.explanation: Matching nested structures function calls and expression parsing.

modules.title: Linked List Patterns

sections.heading: Linked List Essentials
sections.content: Fast and slow pointers detect cycles find middle and solve many linked list problems.
sections.tip: Reverse linked list and detect cycle are asked in every product company interview. Practice until you can write them in 2 minutes without thinking.

key_points: Fast slow pointers for cycle detection
Slow pointer at middle when fast reaches end
Reverse by changing next pointers
Dummy node simplifies edge cases
Merge sorted lists with two pointers

module_quiz.question: Detect cycle uses?
module_quiz.explanation: If fast catches slow they meet inside the cycle.

module_quiz.question: Find middle node?
module_quiz.explanation: When fast reaches end slow is at middle.

module_quiz.question: Reverse linked list changes?
module_quiz.explanation: Each node's next points to previous instead of next.

module_quiz.question: Dummy node purpose?
options: Simplifies edge cases like empty list
module_quiz.explanation: Avoids special handling for head node.

module_quiz.question: Merge sorted lists time?
options: O(N+M) where N M are list lengths
module_quiz.explanation: Visit each node once from both lists.

modules.title: Binary Search

sections.heading: Binary Search Patterns
sections.content: Divide search space in half each step. O(log N). Works on sorted data. Many variations: find exact find first find boundary.
sections.tip: Binary search on answer is powerful. When problem asks find minimum/maximum that satisfies condition — binary search on the answer space.

key_points: O(log N) by halving search space
Works on sorted or monotonic data
left <= right for standard search
Binary search on answer for optimization
Rotated array check which half is sorted

module_quiz.question: Binary search time?
module_quiz.explanation: Halves search space each step.

module_quiz.question: Binary search requires?
module_quiz.explanation: Must be able to eliminate half based on comparison.

module_quiz.question: Find first occurrence?
options: When found go left to check earlier
module_quiz.explanation: Save result and continue searching left for earlier occurrence.

module_quiz.question: Rotated sorted array?
options: Check which half is sorted search accordingly
module_quiz.explanation: One half is always sorted. Check if target is in sorted half.

module_quiz.question: Binary search on answer?
options: Search answer space for min/max satisfying condition
module_quiz.explanation: When answer is monotonic binary search the possible answer range.

modules.title: Tree Patterns

sections.heading: Tree Traversal and DFS
sections.content: Trees are hierarchical. DFS explores depth first. BFS explores level by level. Most tree problems use recursion.
sections.tip: Most tree problems follow this pattern: base case (null node) then process left and right subtrees recursively. If you need level info use BFS with queue.

key_points: Inorder gives sorted order for BST
DFS uses recursion or stack
BFS uses queue for level order
Base case always check null
Most tree problems are recursive

module_quiz.question: Inorder traversal of BST gives?
module_quiz.explanation: Left-Root-Right visits nodes in sorted order for BST.

module_quiz.question: Max depth uses?
module_quiz.explanation: Recursive: depth is 1 plus the deeper subtree.

module_quiz.question: BFS traversal uses?
module_quiz.explanation: Queue processes nodes level by level FIFO.

module_quiz.question: Tree base case?
module_quiz.explanation: When node is null stop recursion and return base value.

module_quiz.question: Balanced tree means?
options: Height difference of subtrees at most 1
module_quiz.explanation: At every node left and right subtree heights differ by at most 1.

modules.title: Dynamic Programming

sections.heading: DP Fundamentals
sections.content: Break problem into overlapping subproblems. Store results to avoid recomputation. Bottom-up (tabulation) or top-down (memoization).
sections.tip: DP approach: 1) Can I break into subproblems? 2) Do subproblems overlap? 3) Define state and transition. 4) Build bottom-up table. Climbing stairs coin change and LCS are the three most asked DP problems.

key_points: DP = overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
Memoization top-down tabulation bottom-up
Define state clearly then find transition
Climbing stairs = Fibonacci
Coin change and LCS are most asked

module_quiz.question: DP vs recursion?
module_quiz.explanation: DP adds memoization to avoid solving same subproblem multiple times.

module_quiz.question: Memoization is?
module_quiz.explanation: Recursive approach that caches results of subproblems.

module_quiz.question: Climbing stairs n=5 same as?
module_quiz.explanation: Ways to reach step N = ways(N-1) + ways(N-2). Same recurrence as Fibonacci.

module_quiz.question: Coin change finds?
module_quiz.explanation: For each amount find minimum coins needed using smaller amounts.

module_quiz.question: DP time vs brute force?
options: DP polynomial brute force exponential often
module_quiz.explanation: DP trades space for time. Exponential becomes polynomial.

modules.title: Graph Patterns

sections.heading: Graph Traversal
sections.content: Graphs are nodes connected by edges. DFS explores deep. BFS explores wide. Used for paths connectivity and shortest route.
sections.tip: DFS for connectivity and path finding. BFS for shortest path. Number of Islands is the most asked graph question. Mark visited cells to avoid infinite loops.

key_points: Adjacency list for graph representation
DFS uses recursion/stack goes deep
BFS uses queue finds shortest path
Mark visited to avoid infinite loops
Number of Islands is #1 graph question

module_quiz.question: BFS finds?
module_quiz.explanation: BFS explores level by level so first path found is shortest.

module_quiz.question: DFS uses?
module_quiz.explanation: Goes deep first using call stack or explicit stack.

module_quiz.question: Number of Islands approach?
options: DFS/BFS on each unvisited land cell
module_quiz.explanation: Each DFS/BFS from new land cell discovers one island.

module_quiz.question: Visited set prevents?
module_quiz.explanation: Without marking visited nodes with cycles would loop forever.

module_quiz.question: Adjacency list stores?
options: Each node with list of its neighbors
module_quiz.explanation: Maps each node to array of connected nodes. Space efficient.

modules.title: Sorting & Greedy

sections.heading: Sorting Algorithms
sections.content: Know time complexity of each. Interviewers test understanding not implementation of basic sorts.
sections.tip: Know complexities: O(N²) for bubble/selection/insertion. O(N logN) for merge/quick. Merge sort is stable (preserves order of equal elements). Quick sort is faster in practice.

sections.heading: Greedy Algorithm
sections.content: Make locally optimal choice at each step hoping for global optimum. Works when greedy choice property holds.
sections.tip: Greedy works for interval scheduling coin problems (specific denominations) and some optimization. If greedy doesnt work try DP.

key_points: Merge Sort O(N logN) stable guaranteed
Quick Sort fastest in practice but O(N²) worst
Greedy makes local optimal choices
Sort by end time for interval problems
If greedy fails try dynamic programming

module_quiz.question: Merge Sort time?
module_quiz.explanation: Divide in half merge back. Always N log N guaranteed.

module_quiz.question: Quick Sort worst case?
module_quiz.explanation: Worst when pivot is always smallest/largest. Average is N log N.

module_quiz.question: Stable sort means?
module_quiz.explanation: Two equal elements keep their original relative order after sorting.

module_quiz.question: Greedy for intervals?
options: Sort by end time pick non-overlapping
module_quiz.explanation: Earliest ending activity leaves most room for others.

module_quiz.question: Greedy vs DP?
options: Greedy local choice DP considers all subproblems
module_quiz.explanation: Greedy is simpler but doesnt always work. DP guarantees optimal.

quiz.question: Coin change approach?
quiz.explanation: DP builds minimum coins for each amount

quiz.question: LCS stands for?
quiz.explanation: Longest sequence common to both strings

quiz.question: Number of islands?
quiz.explanation: Each new land starts DFS counting one island

quiz.question: Monotonic stack for?
quiz.explanation: Maintains order to find next larger

quiz.question: Quick Sort avg?
quiz.explanation: Average N log N worst N²

quiz.question: DP vs greedy?
options: DP considers all greedy local only
quiz.explanation: DP guaranteed optimal greedy may not be

quiz.question: Counting sort time?
quiz.explanation: Linear when range K is small
